title: Michigan Compiled Laws (MCL) — Court-Practice Reference
description: Key sections of the Michigan Compiled Laws most applicable to court practice, organized by chapter and subject matter. The MCL is the official codification of Michigan statutory law.
note: The MCL contains thousands of individual statutes. This reference covers the chapters and sections most frequently encountered in court practice and litigation.

chapters.title: Revised Judicature Act of 1961 (RJA)
chapters.description: The primary statute governing the structure, jurisdiction, and procedure of Michigan courts.

sections.title: Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the short title 'Revised Judicature Act of 1961' (RJA).

sections.title: One Court of Justice
sections.summary: Establishes the 'One Court of Justice' system as required by Mich. Const. 1963, art. VI, § 1.

sections.title: Supreme Court; Jurisdiction
sections.summary: Grants the Supreme Court general superintending control over all inferior courts.

sections.title: Supreme Court; Composition; Term
sections.summary: Supreme Court consists of 7 justices elected on a nonpartisan ballot to 8-year terms.

sections.title: Supreme Court; Quorum; Decision
sections.summary: Four justices constitute a quorum; decision requires concurrence of a majority of justices sitting.

sections.title: Supreme Court; Promulgation of Rules
sections.summary: Supreme Court has power to promulgate rules of procedure and evidence for all courts.

sections.title: Court of Appeals; Establishment
sections.summary: Establishes the Michigan Court of Appeals as an intermediate appellate court.

sections.title: Court of Appeals; Jurisdiction
sections.summary: COA has jurisdiction on appeals of right from final judgments and orders of circuit, probate, and other courts.

sections.title: Circuit Court; Establishment
sections.summary: Establishes circuit courts in each county; circuit court is the court of general jurisdiction.

sections.title: Circuit Court; General Jurisdiction
sections.summary: Circuit courts have original jurisdiction over all civil claims exceeding $25,000 and over all matters in equity.

sections.title: Circuit Court; Family Division
sections.summary: Establishes the Family Division of the Circuit Court with jurisdiction over domestic relations, juvenile, and adoption matters.

sections.title: District Court; Establishment
sections.summary: Establishes the district court system; districts defined by statute.

sections.title: District Court; Jurisdiction
sections.summary: District court has exclusive original jurisdiction over civil actions where amount in controversy does not exceed $25,000.

sections.title: District Court; Small Claims Division
sections.summary: Small claims jurisdiction up to $7,000 ($10,000 for sole proprietors); informal procedure; no attorneys.

sections.title: District Court; Landlord-Tenant
sections.summary: District court has exclusive jurisdiction over summary proceedings to recover possession of premises (eviction).

sections.title: Probate Court; Establishment
sections.summary: Establishes a probate court in each county.

sections.title: Probate Court; Jurisdiction
sections.summary: Probate court has jurisdiction over estates of decedents, trusts, mental health proceedings, guardianships, and conservatorships.

sections.title: Venue; Generally
sections.summary: General venue rules for civil actions; proper county for filing based on defendant's residence or where cause of action arose.

sections.title: Change of Venue
sections.summary: Grounds for change of venue: prejudice, convenience of witnesses, interests of justice.

sections.title: Summons; Issuance
sections.summary: Requirements for issuance of summons to commence a civil action.

sections.title: Service of Process; Personal Service
sections.summary: Methods for personal service of process on individuals, corporations, and other entities.

sections.title: Subpoenas; Issuance
sections.summary: Courts may issue subpoenas to compel attendance of witnesses and production of documents.

sections.title: Actions; Commencement
sections.summary: A civil action is commenced by filing a complaint.

sections.title: Pleadings; Verification
sections.summary: Verification requirements for certain pleadings.

sections.title: Counterclaims and Cross-Claims
sections.summary: Authorization for counterclaims against opposing party and cross-claims against co-parties.

sections.title: Physician-Patient Privilege
sections.summary: A physician shall not disclose confidential information obtained in the course of the professional relationship.

sections.title: Attorney-Client Privilege (Codified)
sections.summary: An attorney shall not disclose communications made to the attorney by a client.

sections.title: Clergy-Penitent Privilege
sections.summary: A minister of the gospel shall not disclose confidential communications made to the minister in the minister's professional capacity.

sections.title: Husband-Wife Privilege
sections.summary: Neither spouse is compelled to testify against the other in a criminal proceeding.

sections.title: Nurse-Patient Privilege
sections.summary: A registered nurse shall not disclose information acquired in the nurse-patient relationship.

sections.title: Social Worker Privilege
sections.summary: A licensed social worker shall not disclose confidential information obtained in the professional relationship.

sections.title: Accountant-Client Privilege
sections.summary: A certified public accountant shall not disclose information obtained in the professional relationship.

sections.title: Psychologist-Patient Privilege
sections.summary: A licensed psychologist shall not disclose confidential information obtained in the professional relationship.

sections.title: Jurors; Challenge for Cause
sections.summary: Grounds for challenging a juror for cause, including interest, relationship, and bias.

sections.title: Peremptory Challenges
sections.summary: Number of peremptory challenges in civil and criminal cases.

sections.title: Costs; Generally
sections.summary: Prevailing party in a civil action is entitled to court costs.

sections.title: Frivolous Claims; Sanctions
sections.summary: Court shall award reasonable costs to a party who prevails against a frivolous claim or defense.

sections.title: Judgments; Generally
sections.summary: General provisions governing entry and enforcement of judgments.

sections.title: Judgment Liens on Real Property
sections.summary: A money judgment becomes a lien on real property in the county where docketed.

sections.title: Small Claims; Monetary Limit
sections.summary: Establishes the monetary jurisdictional limit for small claims court.

sections.title: Interest on Money Judgments
sections.summary: Sets the rate of pre-judgment and post-judgment interest on money judgments.

sections.title: Execution on Judgments
sections.summary: Authorizes writs of execution to collect on money judgments.

sections.title: Garnishment
sections.summary: Authorizes garnishment of wages, bank accounts, and other property to satisfy judgments.

chapters.title: Estates and Protected Individuals Code (EPIC)
chapters.description: Governs wills, trusts, estates, guardianships, and conservatorships in Michigan.

sections.title: Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes short title 'Estates and Protected Individuals Code' (EPIC), effective April 1, 2000.

sections.title: Definitions
sections.summary: Key definitions: decedent, devisee, distributee, domicile, fiduciary, guardian, incapacitated individual, intestate, personal representative, and trust.

sections.title: Intestate Succession; Generally
sections.summary: Rules for distributing estate assets when a person dies without a will (intestate succession).

sections.title: Share of Surviving Spouse
sections.summary: Surviving spouse's share of the intestate estate, which varies based on whether the decedent had children from a prior relationship.

sections.title: Elective Share of Surviving Spouse
sections.summary: A surviving spouse may elect against the will to take an elective share of the augmented estate.

sections.title: Exempt Property and Allowances
sections.summary: Protections for surviving spouse and minor children including homestead allowance, exempt property, and family allowance.

sections.title: Requirements for Execution of Will
sections.summary: A valid Michigan will must be: in writing; signed by the testator; witnessed by two individuals who sign in the testator's conscious presence.

sections.title: Holographic Wills
sections.summary: A will is valid as a holographic will if it is dated and signed by the testator in the testator's handwriting.

sections.title: Revocation of Will
sections.summary: A will is revoked by a subsequent will revoking the prior will, or by physical act of destruction.

sections.title: Probate Jurisdiction of Court
sections.summary: Jurisdiction of probate court over decedents' estates.

sections.title: Informal Probate and Appointment Proceedings
sections.summary: Procedures for informal (unsupervised) probate of wills and appointment of personal representatives.

sections.title: Formal Testacy and Appointment Proceedings
sections.summary: Procedures for formal (supervised) probate and appointment proceedings.

sections.title: Personal Representative; Duties
sections.summary: A personal representative is a fiduciary who must act reasonably for the benefit of the interested persons.

sections.title: Claims Against Estates
sections.summary: Creditors must file claims within 4 months of the date of the first publication of the notice to creditors.

sections.title: Guardianship of Minors
sections.summary: Establishes jurisdiction and procedures for appointing guardians for minor children.

sections.title: Guardianship of Incapacitated Individual
sections.summary: Establishes jurisdiction and procedures for appointing guardians for incapacitated adults.

sections.title: Protective Proceedings; Conservatorship
sections.summary: Establishes procedures for appointing conservators to manage finances of protected individuals.

sections.title: Michigan Trust Code
sections.summary: Governs the creation, administration, modification, and termination of trusts in Michigan.

chapters.title: Child Custody and Parenting Time
chapters.description: Governs child custody determinations, parenting time, and the Friend of the Court system.

sections.title: Child Custody Act; Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the 'Child Custody Act of 1970.'

sections.title: Best Interests of the Child; Factors
sections.summary: Lists the 12 statutory factors courts must consider when determining custody: love and affection; capacity to provide material needs; length of time child has lived in a stable environment; permanence of existing family unit; moral fitness; mental and physical health; performance in school; home, school, and community record; preference of child; willingness to facilitate relationship with other parent; domestic violence; any other relevant factor.

sections.title: Custody Orders; Modification
sections.summary: Court may enter and modify custody orders; party seeking modification must first show a change in circumstances or proper cause.

sections.title: Parental Rights; Presumption
sections.summary: If the parents agree on custody, the court must follow their agreement unless contrary to best interests. If they disagree, the court determines custody based on best interests.

sections.title: Joint Custody
sections.summary: Court may award joint legal or joint physical custody; factors to consider; parents' ability to cooperate.

sections.title: Parenting Time; Enforcement
sections.summary: Parenting time must be granted in frequency, duration, and type as will promote a strong parent-child relationship.

sections.title: Grandparenting Time
sections.summary: Grandparents may petition for grandparenting time if it is in the best interests of the child and parental rights are not terminated.

sections.title: Custody; Standard of Review
sections.summary: Circuit court decisions on custody are reviewed under three standards: de novo (legal questions), clear error (findings of fact), and abuse of discretion (custody award).

sections.title: Change of Domicile
sections.summary: A parent with sole or joint physical custody may not move a child's residence more than 100 miles from the other parent without court approval or other parent's consent.

sections.title: Child Abuse and Neglect; Definitions
sections.summary: Defines child abuse and neglect for purposes of mandatory reporting and child protective proceedings.

sections.title: Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse
sections.summary: Mandates certain professionals (teachers, doctors, social workers, clergy) to report suspected child abuse to DHHS.

chapters.title: Divorce and Separate Maintenance
chapters.description: Governs grounds for divorce, property division, and spousal support in Michigan.

sections.title: Divorce; Grounds
sections.summary: The sole ground for divorce in Michigan is that there has been a breakdown of the marriage relationship to the extent that the objects of matrimony have been destroyed and there remains no reasonable likelihood that the marriage can be preserved.

sections.title: Divorce; Residency Requirement
sections.summary: At least one party must have been a resident of Michigan for 6 months and a resident of the county for 10 days before filing.

sections.title: Divorce; 60-Day Waiting Period
sections.summary: A divorce judgment cannot be entered less than 60 days (or 6 months if minor children) after the complaint is filed.

sections.title: Property Division
sections.summary: The court shall award each party their property and may apportion the marital estate. Michigan is an equitable distribution state.

sections.title: Spousal Support (Alimony)
sections.summary: The court may award spousal support as it considers just and reasonable considering the ability of either party to pay and the character and situation of the parties.

sections.title: Child Support; Uniform Support Orders
sections.summary: Child support must be ordered using the Michigan Child Support Formula; uniform support orders.

sections.title: Income Withholding for Support
sections.summary: Income withholding is automatic in all cases where child support is ordered.

sections.title: Personal Protection Orders (PPO); Domestic Violence
sections.summary: Authorizes ex parte and after-hearing personal protection orders for domestic violence victims.

chapters.title: Michigan Penal Code
chapters.description: Defines criminal offenses and penalties in Michigan.

sections.title: Michigan Penal Code; Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the short title 'Michigan Penal Code.'

sections.title: Felony; Definition
sections.summary: A felony is an offense for which the offender may be punished by imprisonment in a state prison.

sections.title: Misdemeanor; Definition
sections.summary: A misdemeanor is a violation of a penal law of this state that is not a felony or a violation of an ordinance.

sections.title: Assault and Battery
sections.summary: Assault or assault and battery is a misdemeanor punishable by up to 93 days in jail.

sections.title: Aggravated Assault
sections.summary: Assault inflicting serious or aggravated injury without a weapon; misdemeanor up to 1 year.

sections.title: Felonious Assault
sections.summary: Assault with a weapon; felony up to 4 years.

sections.title: Assault with Intent to Commit Murder
sections.summary: Felony punishable by life or any term of years.

sections.title: Attempted Murder
sections.summary: Attempt to commit murder; felony punishable by life or any term of years.

sections.title: Home Invasion
sections.summary: Three degrees of home invasion; first degree is a 20-year felony; second degree is a 15-year felony.

sections.title: Child Abuse
sections.summary: Four degrees of child abuse; first degree is a life felony if intentional harm causing serious physical harm.

sections.title: Conspiracy
sections.summary: Conspiracy to commit a crime is punishable by the same penalty as the underlying offense.

sections.title: Racketeering (RICO)
sections.summary: Michigan's organized crime statute (RICO equivalent); 20-year felony and civil forfeiture.

sections.title: Carrying a Concealed Weapon
sections.summary: Carrying a concealed weapon without a valid CPL; 5-year felony.

sections.title: First Degree Murder
sections.summary: Premeditated murder, murder by poison, lying in wait, killing during commission of enumerated felonies (felony murder); mandatory life without parole.

sections.title: Second Degree Murder
sections.summary: Murder not in the first degree; life or any term of years.

sections.title: Manslaughter
sections.summary: Voluntary or involuntary manslaughter; up to 15 years.

sections.title: Larceny
sections.summary: Taking and carrying away property of another with intent to steal. Amount determines grade: under $200 = misdemeanor; $200-$999 = misdemeanor; $1,000-$19,999 = felony; $20,000+ = 10-year felony.

sections.title: Criminal Sexual Conduct, First Degree (CSC-I)
sections.summary: Sexual penetration under enumerated circumstances (victim under 13, weapon, multiple persons, etc.); life or any term of years; mandatory minimum 25 years if victim under 13.

sections.title: Criminal Sexual Conduct, Second Degree (CSC-II)
sections.summary: Sexual contact (not penetration) under circumstances listed for CSC-I; up to 15 years.

sections.title: Criminal Sexual Conduct, Third Degree (CSC-III)
sections.summary: Sexual penetration under other specified circumstances; up to 15 years.

sections.title: Criminal Sexual Conduct, Fourth Degree (CSC-IV)
sections.summary: Sexual contact under other specified circumstances; up to 2 years.

sections.title: Michigan Anti-Terrorism Act
sections.summary: Defines terrorism and terrorist acts; enhances penalties for terrorist offenses.

chapters.title: Code of Criminal Procedure — Indictment, Plea, and Trial
chapters.description: Governs the procedures for indictment, arraignment, plea, and trial in criminal cases.

sections.title: Code of Criminal Procedure; Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the short title 'Code of Criminal Procedure.'

sections.title: Preliminary Examination; Right
sections.summary: A person charged with a felony is entitled to a preliminary examination before a district judge.

sections.title: Limitations on Prosecution; Statutes of Limitations
sections.summary: General criminal statutes of limitations: murder (no limit); most felonies (6 years); misdemeanors (6 years for serious, 2 years for others).

sections.title: Indictment; Sufficient Description of Offense
sections.summary: An indictment or information is sufficient if it states the offense in the language of the statute.

sections.title: Information; Contents
sections.summary: Formal requirements for the information: name of accused, specific charge, statute violated, date range, county.

chapters.title: Code of Criminal Procedure — Judgment and Sentence
chapters.description: Governs sentencing, probation, and parole in Michigan.

sections.title: Sentencing; Jurisdiction
sections.summary: Court's authority to impose sentences including imprisonment, probation, fines, and costs.

sections.title: Court Costs; Assessment at Sentencing
sections.summary: Authorizes circuit courts to assess court costs at sentencing; costs must be reasonably related to the case.

sections.title: Indeterminate Sentences; Felonies
sections.summary: Michigan uses indeterminate sentencing for felonies: minimum set by judge (within guidelines), maximum set by statute.

sections.title: Life Sentences; Mandatory and Discretionary
sections.summary: Governs imposition of mandatory life sentences (first degree murder) and discretionary life sentences.

sections.title: Habitual Offender; Second Felony Conviction
sections.summary: A person convicted of a felony who has a prior felony conviction may be sentenced as a habitual offender with enhanced penalties.

sections.title: Habitual Offender; Third Felony Conviction
sections.summary: Third-felony habitual offender enhancement; maximum sentence enhanced to life or any term.

sections.title: Habitual Offender; Fourth or Subsequent Felony Conviction
sections.summary: Fourth-felony habitual offender; maximum sentence is life.

sections.title: Michigan Sentencing Guidelines; Applicability
sections.summary: Michigan's legislative sentencing guidelines apply to all felony sentences; guidelines are advisory following Lockridge.

sections.title: Sentencing Guidelines; Departure
sections.summary: Court may depart from guidelines minimum; must articulate substantial and compelling reasons (pre-Lockridge) or any reasonable reason (post-Lockridge).

sections.title: Pre-Sentence Investigation Report
sections.summary: Court shall order a pre-sentence investigation and report before sentencing a defendant convicted of a felony.

chapters.title: Mental Health Code
chapters.description: Governs voluntary and involuntary mental health treatment, hospitalization, and related proceedings.

sections.title: Mental Health Code; Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the short title 'Mental Health Code.'

sections.title: Definitions
sections.summary: Defines: clinical certificate, customer, department, hospital, mental illness, and mental health professional.

sections.title: Voluntary Admission
sections.summary: An individual 18 or older may request voluntary admission to a hospital for mental health treatment.

sections.title: Clinical Certificate; Involuntary Treatment
sections.summary: A mental health professional may execute a clinical certificate for involuntary examination if a person has a mental illness and is a danger to self or others.

sections.title: Petition for Hospitalization
sections.summary: An individual may petition the probate court for the hospitalization of another person who is mentally ill and a danger.

sections.title: Hearing on Hospitalization Petition
sections.summary: Probate court must hold a hearing within 7 days of receipt of petition; right to counsel; standard is clear and convincing evidence.

sections.title: Assisted Outpatient Treatment
sections.summary: Court may order assisted outpatient treatment as an alternative to inpatient hospitalization.

chapters.title: Tort Liability and Civil Actions
chapters.description: Key tort reform statutes governing civil liability in Michigan.

sections.title: Medical Malpractice; Standard of Care
sections.summary: A medical malpractice plaintiff must establish the appropriate standard of care and that the defendant failed to meet it.

sections.title: Medical Malpractice; Notice of Intent
sections.summary: Before filing a medical malpractice claim, plaintiff must file a Notice of Intent (NOI) and wait 182 days.

sections.title: Products Liability; Seller Liability
sections.summary: A product seller (not manufacturer) may be liable only if the seller is also a manufacturer, was negligent, made express warranty, or altered the product.

sections.title: Products Liability; Defenses
sections.summary: Defenses available to product manufacturers and sellers, including state-of-the-art defense.

sections.title: Pure Comparative Fault
sections.summary: Michigan uses a modified comparative fault rule: plaintiff may recover if less than 51% at fault; recovery reduced proportionally.

sections.title: Allocation of Fault; Several Liability
sections.summary: In tort cases, damages are allocated among all tortfeasors based on their percentage of fault; joint liability applies in certain circumstances.

sections.title: Governmental Immunity; Highway Exception
sections.summary: Governmental agencies are generally immune from tort liability; highway exception allows claims for failure to maintain roads in reasonable repair.

sections.title: Governmental Immunity; Motor Vehicle Exception
sections.summary: A governmental agency is liable for bodily injury or property damage resulting from the negligent operation of a government-owned motor vehicle.

sections.title: Individual Governmental Immunity
sections.summary: A government employee is not liable for an act that is the result of a discretionary-decisional, as opposed to ministerial-operational, governmental function, if they did not act in bad faith.

sections.title: Statutes of Limitations; Personal Injury
sections.summary: Most personal injury claims must be filed within 3 years of the date of injury.

sections.title: Medical Malpractice; Statute of Limitations
sections.summary: Medical malpractice claims must be filed within 2 years of the act or omission giving rise to the claim.

sections.title: Disability; Tolling of Limitations
sections.summary: Limitations periods are tolled for individuals who are a minor or insane at the time the claim arises.

chapters.chapter: MCL Chapter 600 — Family Division and Juvenile Code
chapters.title: Juvenile Code
chapters.description: Governs proceedings in the Family Division of Circuit Court involving minors.

sections.title: Juvenile Code; Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the short title 'Juvenile Code.'

sections.title: Juvenile Court; Jurisdiction
sections.summary: Family Division has exclusive original jurisdiction over proceedings involving minors: abuse/neglect (b jurisdiction), delinquency (a jurisdiction), and status offenses (d jurisdiction).

sections.title: Criminal Jurisdiction Over Juveniles (Waiver)
sections.summary: Court may waive jurisdiction over a juvenile 14 or older charged with a serious felony and allow prosecution as an adult.

sections.title: Dispositions; Juvenile Delinquency
sections.summary: Dispositional options following adjudication: probation, placement, community service, restitution, and commitment to MDHHS.

sections.title: Termination of Parental Rights; Grounds
sections.summary: Statutory grounds for terminating parental rights including abandonment, abuse, neglect, and felony conviction.

sections.title: Nonpartisan Judicial Elections
sections.summary: Judges at all levels in Michigan are elected on a nonpartisan ballot.

sections.title: Judicial Campaign Conduct
sections.summary: Governs campaign conduct for judicial candidates in accordance with the Michigan Code of Judicial Conduct.

chapters.title: Michigan Vehicle Code — Traffic and Civil Infractions
chapters.description: Governs traffic laws, civil infractions, and related proceedings in district court.

sections.title: Operating While Intoxicated (OWI)
sections.summary: Operating a motor vehicle while intoxicated (BAC 0.08 or more, or under the influence of controlled substance). First offense: misdemeanor up to 93 days. Second offense: 1 year. Third offense: felony 1-5 years.

sections.title: Implied Consent
sections.summary: A person who operates a vehicle on Michigan roads impliedly consents to chemical testing for alcohol or controlled substances.

sections.title: Civil Infraction; Defined
sections.summary: A civil infraction is a violation of law that is not a crime; no imprisonment may be imposed; civil fines apply.

chapters.title: Business Organizations
chapters.description: Governs formation and operation of business entities in Michigan.

sections.title: Business Corporation Act; Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the short title 'Business Corporation Act' (BCA) governing Michigan for-profit corporations.

sections.title: Limited Liability Company Act; Short Title
sections.summary: Establishes the short title 'Michigan Limited Liability Company Act.'

sections.title: Uniform Partnership Act
sections.summary: Governs general partnerships in Michigan; rights and liabilities of partners.
